coredata.dc:title: Disposable electrochemical sensors for hemoglobin detection based on ferrocenoyl cysteine conjugates modified electrode
coredata.dc:creator: Han, Guo-Cheng
Su, Xiaorui
Hou, Jiating
Ferranco, Annaleizle
Feng, Xiao-Zhen
Zeng, Ruosheng
Chen, Zhencheng
Kraatz, Heinz-Bernhard
coredata.dc:description: The abnormal content of hemoglobin in human body can lead to several diseases such as anemia, thalassemia, leukemia, heart disease. Therefore, it is very important for sensitive detection of hemoglobin in clinical analysis. An electrochemical approach is presented for detecting hemoglobin using disposable electrochemical sensors, which were based on the ability of the redox active ferrocene group of Fc[CO-Cys(Trt)-OMe]2 and Fc[CO-Glu-Cys-Gly-OH]. The two electroactive materials were adsorbed onto gold nanoparticles that were previously electrochemically deposited onto screen-printed electrodes. The as-prepared sensors were characterized by scanning electron microscopy and energy dispersive spectroscopy, and their electrochemical properties were characterized by cyclic voltammetry, differential pulse voltammetry and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy. Results indicated the Fc[CO-Glu-Cys-Gly-OH] prepared sensor showed enhanced current responses for hemoglobin catalysis than the Fc[CO-Cys(Trt)-OMe]2 prepared sensor. Optimal operating conditions were determined for hemoglobin detection by differential pulse voltammetry. The concentration of hemoglobin showed a good linear relationship with the current difference in the range of 0.1–1000 μg/mL and with 0.03 μg/mL limit of detection. The development of this electrochemical sensor was used for hemoglobin detection in human serum with a R.S.D. lower than 4.7% and recovery between 95.5% and 103.2%, which was in agreement with the data obtained from typical clinical analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemical sensors
Hemoglobin
Detection
Ferrocene
Clinical analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Hb detection by electrochemical sensors and the sensitivity, selectivity, repeatability and reproducibility study

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fabrication of Fc(Cys)2/AuNPs/SPE and Fc-ECG/AuNPs/SPE electrochemical sensors for hemoglobin detection by DPV.

SEM images of the surface morphologies of A: SPE; B: AuNPs/SPE; C: Fc(Cys)2/AuNPs/SPE; D:Fc-ECG/AuNPs/SPE, Fig. 1B presents many uniform spherical gold nanoparticles about 55–80 nm, Fig. 1C forms a remarkably smooth film with drop of Fc(Cys)2, Fig. 1D shows enlarged approximately 200 nm particles with drop of Fc-ECG.

Cyclic voltammagrams of a:SPE; b:AuNPs/SPE; c:Fc(Cys)2/AuNPs/SPE; d:Hb/Fc(Cys)2/AuNPs/SPE; e:Fc-ECG/AuNPs/SPE; f:Hb/Fc-ECG/AuNPs/SPE). Results were collected with a scan rate of 0.1 V·s−1 in aqueous solution with 5.0 mmol/L K4[Fe(CN)]6/K3[Fe(CN)]6 containing 0.1 mol/L KCl as the supporting electrolyte.

DPVs of electrochemical sensors in a 0.2 mol/L phosphate buffer (PBS, pH = 7.0) with a pulse amplitude of 50 mV in range of -200-800 mV (b:AuNPs/SPE; c:Fc(Cys)2/AuNPs/SPE; d:Hb/Fc(Cys)2/AuNPs/SPE; e:Fc-ECG/AuNPs/SPE; f:Hb/Fc-ECG/AuNPs/SPE).

A: DPV for different concentrations of hemoglobin. Inset: Calibration plot for various concentrations of hemoglobin; B: The selectivity of Fc-ECG/AuNPs/SPE for hemoglobin, HSA, glucose, ascorbic acid, IgG and DL-cysteine, the concentration of each compound is 300 μg/mL; C: The repeatability of Fc(Cys)2/AuNPs/SPE and Fc-ECG/AuNPs/SPE sensors; D:The reproducibility of Fc(Cys)2/AuNPs/SPE and Fc-ECG/AuNPs/SPE sensors.

head.ce:title: Disposable electrochemical sensors for hemoglobin detection based on ferrocenoyl cysteine conjugates modified electrode

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Life and Environmental Sciences, Guilin University of Electronic Technology, Guilin 541004, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Life and Environmental Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physical and Environmental Sciences, University of Toronto Scarborough, Toronto, Ontario M1C 1A4, Canada

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Physical and Environmental Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The abnormal content of hemoglobin in human body can lead to several diseases such as anemia, thalassemia, leukemia, heart disease. Therefore, it is very important for sensitive detection of hemoglobin in clinical analysis. An electrochemical approach is presented for detecting hemoglobin using disposable electrochemical sensors, which were based on the ability of the redox active ferrocene group of Fc[CO-Cys(Trt)-OMe]2 and Fc[CO-Glu-Cys-Gly-OH]. The two electroactive materials were adsorbed onto gold nanoparticles that were previously electrochemically deposited onto screen-printed electrodes. The as-prepared sensors were characterized by scanning electron microscopy and energy dispersive spectroscopy, and their electrochemical properties were characterized by cyclic voltammetry, differential pulse voltammetry and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy. Results indicated the Fc[CO-Glu-Cys-Gly-OH] prepared sensor showed enhanced current responses for hemoglobin catalysis than the Fc[CO-Cys(Trt)-OMe]2 prepared sensor. Optimal operating conditions were determined for hemoglobin detection by differential pulse voltammetry. The concentration of hemoglobin showed a good linear relationship with the current difference in the range of 0.1–1000 μg/mL and with 0.03 μg/mL limit of detection. The development of this electrochemical sensor was used for hemoglobin detection in human serum with a R.S.D. lower than 4.7% and recovery between 95.5% and 103.2%, which was in agreement with the data obtained from typical clinical analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Most organisms require molecular oxygen (O2) in order to live. O2 binds to Fe(II) coordinated by a tetramer known as Hemoglobin (Hb). Hb is composed of four protein chains, two α and two β chains, which is used for transport of O2 and O2 binds to Fe(II) in Hb [1]. Malfunctioning Hb can lead to several diseases such as anemia, thalassemia, leukemia, heart disease [2,3]. Therefore, an ideal method for sensitive detection of Hb can find great use for clinical analysis [4].

Many techniques were used for Hb detection, including a specific gravity approach [5,6], colorimetric methods [7,8], spectrophotometry [9], fluorescent spectrometry [10], Kurt electric resistance method [11], and electrochemical methods [12–14]. However, the most advantageous approach is electrochemical analysis due to its high sensitivity, portability, low-cost instrumentation, rapid detection, and easy automation [15,16]. Direct electro-oxidation of Hb is problematic as the heme center is deeply embedded into the protein leading to difficulties involving electron transfer between the electrode surface and the large protein [17]. In addition, protein adsorption onto the electrode surface would initiate denaturation of Hb and passivation of the electrode. Additionally, the electrical signal is easily shielded by charge current, residual current and other background current [18].

The introduction of nano-materials into electrochemical biosensors can improve the sensitivity, selectivity and reproducibility of biosensors. With the rapid development of nanotechnology, some nano-materials, such as magnetic nanoparticles are biocompatible [19–24], and have been used to modify electrode surfaces to increase surface area [25,26]. Varying nano-materials have been used such as Pt and gold nanoparticles (NPs) [27–29], PVP/CdS quantum dot [30,31], to modify the electrode surface for direct determination of Hb in blood.

Ferrocene possesses ideal electrochemical properties as a metal organic complex, which can be used for electrochemical detection [32], it can be readily modified with biomolecules and is stable in aqueous media [33]. A variety of ferrocene derivatives have been shown to be suitable recognition receptors, which have good redox properties, and interact with various enzymes, DNA, RNA and other macromolecular substances in cells [34,35]. Ferrocene boric acid was designed as an electrochemical probe that was bound to the N-terminus of glycated Hb and used to detect hemoglobin, which showed reversible redox peaks at 0.29 V [31]. Ferrocene carboxaldehyde was covalently attached to anti-human Hb monoclonal antibody to detect Hb, a reversible redox peak was observed at 0.35 V when reacting with Hb [36].

In this study, screen-printed electrodes (SPEs) were used as the substrate, gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) were deposited onto the surface of substrate by potentiostatic deposition. Two electroactive materials Fc[CO-Cys(Trt)-OMe]2 (abbreviation as Fc(Cys)2), and Fc[CO-Glu-Cys-Gly-OH] (abbreviation as Fc-ECG) were self-assembled onto the surface of AuNPs by covalent bonds (Au-S) to prepare two modified electrodes Fc(Cys)2/AuNPs/SPE and Fc-ECG/AuNPs/SPE, which were investigated by cyclic voltammetry (CV), differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) and electrochemical impendence spectroscopy (EIS), where Fc-ECG/AuNPs/SPE exhibited higher selectivity and excellent sensitivity for Hb catalysis by the DPV technique and therefore, the direct electrochemical method for Hb detection was achieved.

Fc(Cys)2 was synthesized according to the following reference [37]. Fc-ECG was purchased from Chang Xi Biotechnology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai China), human Hb and human serum albumin (HSA) were purchased from Purple Industry Chemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai China), chloroauric acid (HAuCl4) was purchased from SANGON biological Co. Ltd. (Shanghai China), sulphuric acid (H2SO4) and sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3) were purchased from Guilin Xinpeng Chemical Reagent Sales Department, immunoglobulin E (IgE) was purchased from Dingguo Changsheng Biotechnology Co. Ltd. (Beijing China), glucose, ascorbic acid, and DL-cysteine were all derived from Aladdin Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai China). Human serum was provided by the People’s Hospital of Guilin, which was directly diluted by PBS (Phosphate-buffered saline) buffer for DPV test. All other reagents were of analytical grade and were used without further purification. All aqueous solutions were prepared with Milli-Q water.

The preparation of electrochemical sensors Fc(Cys)2/AuNPs/SPE and Fc-ECG/AuNPs/SPE for Hb detection are described as shown in Scheme 1 . At first, 0.5 mmol/L of Fc(Cys)2 stock solution was prepared by dissolving Fc(Cys)2 in acetonitrile solvent, 0.5 mmol/L of Fc-ECG stock solution was prepared by dissolving Fc-ECG in Milli-Q water, and 1.0 g/L of Hb stock solution was prepared by dissolving Hb in Milli-Q water. The prepared solutions were stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C. When tested, all the stock solutions were diluted to the appropriate lower concentration solution. Then, the fabrication of the sensors were initially conducted using SPEs, which were activated in 0.5 mol/L of H2SO4 solution by scanning CV 20 cycles with 0.1 V·S−1 in range of -0.2-+1.0 V. After activation, AuNPs were deposited on the surface of SPE which were derived from chloroauric acid directly by potentiostatic deposition for 120 s with -0.5 V potential. Thirdly, electroactive materials different volume of 0.5 mmol/L of Fc(Cys)2 or Fc-ECG were individually dropped on the individual surfaces of each AuNPs/SPE and loaded on the AuNPs forming Au-S covalent bonds through the thiol groups on Fc(Cys)2 0.5 mmol/L of or Fc-ECG for 30 min until dry in nature. Last, different concentration of Hb was dropped on the sensors for a few min until dry in nature for optimization and final test. For reproducibility test of the sensors, they were immersed in 0.5 mol/L of NaHCO3 solution for desorption 10 min and taken up dry in nature, and then for DPV test.

The electrochemical analyses were carried out using SPEs with one modified carbon paste electrode as the working electrode, another carbon paste electrode as the auxiliary electrode and Ag/AgCl electrode as the pseudoreference electrode in an enclosed faraday cage, which were connected to a CHI660E electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Instrument Co., Ltd., China). CV were carried out with a scan rate of 0.1 V·s−1 in aqueous solution with 5.0 mmol/L K4[Fe(CN)]6/K3[Fe(CN)]6 containing 0.1 mol/L KCl as the supporting electrolyte. DPV was performed in a 0.2 mol/L phosphate buffer (PBS, pH = 7.0) with a pulse amplitude of 50 mV in range of −200 to +800 mV.

All EIS measurements were performed with open circuit potentials and with a frequency range from 100,000 to 0.1 Hz with an amplitude of 5 mV and an initial voltage of 0.24 mV. The software ZView 3.1 was used to perform simulations on the EIS results by using a modified Randles’ equivalent circuit.

Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images equipped with an energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) analyzer of the electrochemical sensors were analyzed on field emission scanning electron microscope produced by Japan High Technology Company and England Oxford Company at 3 kV and 10,100 nA with the working distance of 6000 μm.

The aim of this work was to develop a simple and convenient method for the direct electrochemical detection of Hb in human serum. The main idea of the proposed method utilizes ferrocenoyl cysteine conjugates as electroactive materials and Au nanocomposites as electron transfer medium to show an electrical response when interacting with Hb. Following fabrication, SEM images of the electrode surface were obtained as depicted in Fig. 1 .

The surface of the bare electrode was rugged and particle distribution was absent for naked SPE (Fig. 1A), after electrodeposition the surface of the substrate showed enhanced distribution with many spherical particles of uniform shape possessing the size of about 55–80 nm (Fig. 1B), which indicated that AuNPs have been successfully modified on the electrode surface. As shown in Fig. 1C, the accumulation of Fc(Cys)2 onto the surface appeared to form a remarkably smooth film with no cracks [38]. This observation could be attributed to the formation of the two Au-S bonds between the thiol groups of Fc(Cys)2 and AuNPs. However, when a drop of Fc-ECG was applied to the AuNPS it was shown to enlarge the size of the particles to approximately 200 nm (Fig. 1D). As the Au-S bonds were formed between Fc-ECG and AuNPs, which is capable of providing better sensitivity when interacting with Hb than the Fc(Cys)2 modified electrode.

The EDS tests were measured to confirm the preparation of sensor material shown in Fig.S1 and the element content of the sensors surface are shown Table S1. From the Fig.S1 A, except for the common electrode material carbon and nitrogen, sulfur element was observed because that the carbon electrodes were activated H2SO4 solution. When after activation, AuNPs were deposited on the surface of SPE by potentiostatic deposition, so that gold element was found on the electrode surface (Fig.S1B). Subsequently, electroactive materials Fc(Cys)2 and Fc-ECG were individually dropped on the individual surfaces of each AuNPs/SPE and loaded on the AuNPs as discovered by one more element iron on the surface and confirm that forming Au-S covalent bonds through the thiol groups on Fc(Cys)2 and Fc-ECG in Fig.S1C and S1D, respectively. The results can also be obtained from Table S1.

When it comes to developing electrochemical sensors, it is very important to study their electrochemical properties to verify their sensing abilities. The electrochemical behaviors of the sensors were carried out by CV experiments as shown in Fig. 2 .

In comparison to curve a, the peak current for curve b appeared to be enhanced in size and widely separated indicating that a quasi-reversible redox reaction was observed, after a layer of AuNPs were electrochemically deposited on the surface of SPE. This observation is due to the large specific surface area of AuNPs and its improved electrical conductivity, the modification with AuNPs on the electrode surface has resulted in an increase in the electrode surface area, which is directly proportional to the increase in current and allows for enhanced electron transfer between Fe2+/Fe3+ in solution and the electrode surface [39]. Surprisingly, an irreversible redox signal was observed after each electroactive material Fc(Cys)2 (curve c) and Fc-ECG (curve e) were adsorbed individually onto the AuNPs/SPE. Anodic potential shifts were detected for each modified electrode and each of the currents remarkably decreased. When comparing the peak current of Fc-ECG/AuNPs/SPE (curve e), it was greater than that of Fc(Cys)2/AuNPs/SPE (curve c). This observation supports our SEM data as adsorption of Fc(Cys)2 onto AuNPs/SPE decreases the extent of electron transfer through the electrode.

Subsequently, a layer of Hb was added onto the modified electrodes. After the addition of Hb onto Fc(Cys)2/AuNPs/SPE (curve d), the potential changed from 175 mV to 475 mV and the current changed from 100 μA to 25 μA; and after the addition of Hb onto Fc-ECG/AuNPs/SPE (curve f), the potential changed from 175 mV to 280 mV and the current change from 100 μA to 89 μA. However, when we compared the peak current of Hb/Fc-ECG/AuNPs/SPE (curve f) appeared to be greater than that of Hb/Fc(Cys)2/AuNPs/SPE (curve d), indicating that the Fc-ECG/AuNPs/SPE electrochemical sensor is much more sensitive for Hb detection and is consistent with the SEM results.

EIS is a useful technique to examine the stepwise modification processes when changing the electrode interface properties. The EIS signals for each sensor at different construction stages are shown in Fig. S2A, while Fig. S2B (the inset figure depicts the equivalent circuit diagram for EIS analog) are the EIS signals which were fitted data (Fig.S3) by the ZView 3.1 software. In the equivalent circuit diagram, Rs represents the solution resistance, Rp represents the electron transfer resistance on the surface of an electrochemical sensor, and Cdl represents the component capacitance of the electrochemical sensor.

The electron transfer resistance (Rct) of SPE was 11.35 KΩ as shown in curve a, a considerable resistance was present because the unmodified SPE is not suitable for electron transfer. After electrodeposition (curves b), the disappearance of the semicircle suggests that comparatively fast electron transfer kinetics between Fe(CN)]6/K3[Fe(CN)]6 and the electrode surface. AuNPs provide good electrical conductivity, which corresponds well with the CV results. Once electroactive materials Fc(Cys)2 and Fc-ECG were adsorbed onto the AuNPs, a bigger resistances were present for curve c and e when each curve was compared to curve b implying a very high electron transfer resistance to the redox-probe and the block of charge transfer by the formed films. The Fc(Cys)2/AuNPs/SPE modified electrode (curve c), Rct value increased to 14.66 KΩ, while the Fc-ECG/AuNPs/SPE modified electrode (curve e) Rct value increased to 1.78 KΩ. This increase occurred by the formation of a barrier layer of the electroactive molecules resulting in a hindered pathway for electron transfer. The addition of Hb on each Fc(Cys)2/AuNPs/SPE and Fc-ECG/AuNPs/SPE modified electrodes showed that Rct value increased to 182.23 (curve d) and 3.37 KΩ (curve f), correspondingly. The significantly hampered electron transfer indicated that the Fc(Cys)2/AuNPs/SPE electrochemical sensor is not very sensitive for Hb detection, these results are consistent with the CV and SEM results.
DPV was also used to monitor the stepwise modification processes of each Fc(Cys)2/AuNPs/SPE and Fc-ECG/AuNPs/SPE modified sensor surfaces in presence of Hb as shown in Fig. 3 .

As shown in Fig. 3, no current response was observed due to the absence of an electroactive species in this potential range for curve b. Curve c was the response curve of Fc(Cys)2/AuNPs/SPE, which had a maximum current response value of 0.68 μA at 448 mV. When Hb was added (curve d), the sensor showed a maximum current response value of 0.51 μA at 496 mV. The maximum current response value for curve e was 2.22 μA at 120 mV for Fc-ECG/AuNPs/SPE modified electrode, which was attributed to the electrochemical behavior of Fc group on Fc-ECG. When the Hb was added (curve f), the sensor detected a maximum current response value of 0.86 μA at 124 mV, the current peak value of the sensor clearly decreased. The difference in current responses may be due to the formation of stable films by Fc(Cys)2 as shown in Fig. 1C, it cannot easily react with Hb [40]. The Fc-ECG/AuNPs/SPE modified electrode appeared to show higher sensitivity to Hb and therefore, further investigations were carried out by the Fc-ECG/AuNPs/SPE electrochemical sensor.

In order to fabricate an efficient and sensitive sensor, various conditions were monitored three times to investigate the performance of the sensors, such as pH values, different incubation temperatures, various added volumes of Fc-ECG and numerous binding times and the results are shown in Fig. S4.

In Fig. S4 A, the pH values range from 5.7 to 7.5, and the response current of the sensor increases as the pH value increases. When the pH value was greater than 7.0, the response current decreases gradually, and the maximum response current of the sensor was achieved at pH = 7.0. In Fig. S4B, the sensor response current increases as the temperature increases, the response current reaches the maximum value at 25 °C, the response current of the sensor decreases as the temperature increases when above 25 °C. In Fig. S4C, as the Fc-ECG content increases, the response current value of the sensor increases, and when the total of 6 μL of 500 μg/mL Hb was added, the response value nearly stabilizes. In Fig. S4D, when the binding time reached 1 h, the maximum current response for the sensor was achieved. However, less than 1 h of binding time does not allow the Hb to be fixed on the sensor and has not reacted completely with Fc-ECG. At the same time, if the binding time exceeds 1 h, it will reduce the activity of Fc-ECG. Thus, optimal incubation conditions are a pH = 7.0 at 25 °C, with a drop volume of Fc-ECG solution of 10 μL and finally, the preferred binding time of Hb was 1 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hb detection by electrochemical sensors and the sensitivity, selectivity, repeatability and reproducibility study
ce:section.ce:para: The most optimal operating conditions were used for the direct detection of Hb by DPV. The amperometric responses of the Fc-ECG/AuNPs/SPE electrochemical sensor with different concentrations of Hb are shown in Fig. 4 A, the inset graph shows the calibration plots.

It can be seen that the response current of the sensor decreased as the Hb concentration increased. The concentration of Hb and current difference showed a good linear relationship, the linear equation was ΔI = 0.40334 + 0.00109c (ΔI is the current difference, and c is the Hb concentration), linear range was 0.1–1000 μg/mL, correlation coefficient was 0.99824, and the limit of detection was 0.03 μg/mL to show its higher sensitivity [41]. A comparison with other methods for the detection of Hb is shown in Table S2, the proposed method has a higher detection limit and a wider linear range with a suitable assay time.

The possible interference factors of Hb detection were also investigated, 300 μg/mL HSA, glucose, ascorbic acid, IgE and DL-cysteine insteading of Hb were used to study the selectivity of the sensors. The five different types of interfering agents in serum do not cause obvious interference for the detection of Hb as shown in Fig. 4B, indicating that the sensor possesses good selectivity for the detection of Hb.

In addition, the as-prepared Fc-ECG/AuNPs/SPE modified electrode was put in PBS and scanned 6 times, the value of current did not change indicating that electrode owns good repeatability for both sensors of Fc(Cys)2/AuNPs/SPE and Fc-ECG/AuNPs/SPE as shown in Fig. 4C.

It’s very important to evaluate the reproducibility of electrochemical sensors for their actual application [42,43], after the desorption of Hb on the surface of the sensor, the DPV scan was carried out, and the response current signal of Fc(Cys)2/AuNPs/SPE almost do not change as shown in Fig.4D, but for the Fc-ECG modified sensor, the change in current was very sharp. The former sensor produced a thin layer film(Fig. 1C) that is very stable and the adsorption of Hb was difficult. Fc-ECG is a very active molecule and can easily adsorb Hb. The desorption, possibly the whole complex of Fc-ECG and Hb were separated from the electrode, which led to a decreased response current signal. However, it should be pointed out that the sensitivity of Fc-ECG/AuNPs/SPE modified sensor (0.03 μg/mL) is 10 times higher than the sensitivity of Fc(Cys)2/AuNPs/SPE modified sensor (0.3 μg/mL) as shown in Fig.S5.

Meanwhile, to demonstrate the viability of our method to directly assay a real sample, human serum samples were used. The human serum samples were determined by the standard addition method [44], the accuracy of the assay was evaluated by determining the recovery and R.S.D for the spiked sample as shown in Table S3. The Hb content of human serum samples was 101.22 μg/mL, which was detected by present method. The determined value was in agreement with the data obtained from typical clinical analysis (100 μg/mL) suggesting that the sensor that was developed showed suitable precision. The recoveries for assays of the three different added concentrations of Hb varied from 95.5% to 103.2% with a R.S.D. lower than 4.7%. Therefore, the direct electrochemical detection method for Hb detection was achieved.

Disposable electrochemical sensors were constructed by exploiting Fc(Cys)2 and Fc-ECG conjugates and individually, adsorbing them onto AuNPs/SPE to create two types of modified electrodes. Results demonstrated that Fc-ECG modified electrode exhibited ideal sensor abilities, as enhanced current responses were achieved in presence of Hb. The system comprised of Fc-ECG/AuNPs/SPE exhibited a very reliable selectivity and sensitivity. In addition, direct electrochemical detection of Hb was achieved by DPV, which was applied to Hb detection in human serum with a R.S.D. lower than 4.7% and a recovery between 95.5% and 103.2%, which was in line with clinical requirements for Hb analysis.
The authors declare that there are no conflicts of interest in regard to this study.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We appreciate the financial supports from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 61661014, 61627807, 81873913, 21661010, 61301038), the Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi (2015GXNSFBA139041, 2017GXNSFGA198005). Funding from the Natural Science and Engineering Research Council of Canada and the University of Toronto are appreciated.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.11.042.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

Using poly(3-aminophenylboronic acid) thin film with binding-induced ion flux blocking for amperometric detection of hemoglobin A1c
Biosens. Bioelectron.
A novel nitrite biosensor based on the direct electron transfer of hemoglobin immobilized on CdS hollow nanospheres
Biosens. Bioelectron.
A disposable amperometric dual-sensor for the detection of hemoglobin and glycated hemoglobin in a finger prick blood sample
Biosens. Bioelectron.
Electrochemical sensor based on magnetic molecularly imprinted nanoparticles modified magnetic electrode for determination of Hb
Biosens. Bioelectron.
A disposable paper-based microfluidic immunosensor based on reduced graphene oxide-tetraethylene pentamine/Au nanocomposite decorated carbon screen-printed electrodes
Sens. Actuators B-Chem.
Paper-based microfluidic devices for electrochemical immunofiltration analysis of human chorionic gonadotropin
Biosens. Bioelectron.
A sensitive and selective electrochemical biosensor for the determination of beta-amyloid oligomer by inhibiting the peptide-triggered in situ assembly of silver nanoparticles
Int. J. Nanomedicine

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Guo-Cheng Han received his Ph.D. degree in applied chemistry from the Central South University (2011). He is currently a professor of Biomedical Engineering at Guilin University of Electronic Technology. His research interests cover synthesis of nanomaterials and their applications in biosensors and electrochemical analysis.

Xiaorui Su is a postgraduate of Biomedical Engineering in School of Life and Environmental Sciences, Guilin University of Electronic Technology. Her research interests are biomolucules detection and electrochemical analysis.

Jiating Hou received her master's degree in Biomedical Engineering from Guilin University of Electronic Technology in 2018. Her research interests are biomolucules detection and electrochemical analysis.
Annaleizle Ferranco received her Ph.D. degree from University of Toronto Scarborough (2018). Her interests lie in development of electrochemical biosensor for clinical applications.

Xiao-Zhen Feng is currently an associate professor of analytical chemistry at Guilin University of Electronic Technology. Her research interests focus on electrochemical biosensors and surface enhanced Raman scattering.

Ruosheng Zeng received his Ph.D. degree from the Hunan University (2010). He is currently a professor of Biomedical Engineering at Guilin University of Electronic Technology. His research interests include synthesis of nanomaterials and electrical devices.

Zhencheng Chen received his Ph.D. degree in Biomedical Engineering from the Xi'an Jiaotong University (2002). He is currently a professor of Biomedical Engineering at Guilin University of Electronic Technology. His research interests include biosensors and electrical devices.

Heinz-Bernhard Kraatz received his Ph.D. in 1993 from the University of Calgary and carried out postdoctoral work at the University of Maryland. In 1998, he joined the Department of Chemistry at The University of Saskatchewan and was appointed full professor in 2007. In 2011, he accepted a professorship at the University of Toronto. His research interests cover organometallic chemistry, electrochemical biosensor and electrochemical analysis.
